## [00:00 - 03:02]

Welcome to the BPM method Genesis. BPM stands for Brand Driven Performance Marketing. And in this short video, I'm gonna give you an introduction into what's coming up and the transformation that I'm hoping that you're gonna get from this program. Now, why are we here? The big problem is this. Everyone wants to reach their prospects at the lowest possible cost with the highest possible conversion rate and to get them spending as much as you can. At the end of the day, if you're just doing e-commerce, info products, coaching funnels, whatever it is, your goal is to reach your customers as profitably as possible. So that could be lowering ad costs, could be increasing ad conversion, in site conversion rate, could be about getting them to spend more per transaction or spend more over time. And really, when you look at advertising with so much competition on different platforms and various things going on, you want to be in control of as much as possible. So what we are building with you through the BPM method is stability, predictability and profitability. One of the big things about the BPM method is it's based on teachings from over the last 100 years. Various people have gone into creating this through me and through my learning. So I've been in marketing for 20 or so years and everything I've brought into the BPM method is taking constructs of advertising and marketing over the many, many years, 50, 100 years, reading the books, going to seminars, understanding, studying, working with mentors to really get to the bottom of how can I reach my customers profitably regardless of which ad platform I'm going for. And this quote, and there are many that I could bring to you from Clausey Hopkins is quite pertinent to where I think advertisers really struggle. So advertising is salesmanship. Its principles are the principles of salesmanship. The only purpose of advertising is to make sales. Now you could argue both sides of the equation here. One is yes, I agree, and when I run an ad, I want to make a sale. The other one could be, yeah, but I want to run an ad to grow my brand. I want to do something that's going to help me profit in three months times, six months times, 12. It doesn't matter. If you are running ads, you're doing it to generate sales. And that's our ultimate goal. But the way we do that differs based on the type of product, how much it's going to cost. And one of the things I learned over the years is building avatars is one of the easiest shortcuts to finding out how to do that. So this is a guy called David Ogilvy. If you haven't heard of him, he is a big mentor of mine that I've just learned a ton over the last 10, 15 years of how to build avatars and how to build ads

## [03:02 - 06:03]

that are going to appeal to people's needs and desires and putting that into a creative process. So around 1930, David Ogilvy was doing door-to-door sales. So he was a researcher that kind of previously was a chef. And then he started working for a company called Aga Cooker in the US. He's from England, by the way, but he was in the US. He wrote a sales manual after he became Aga Cooker's number one door-to-door salesperson. So this is around 1930 when the US was in depression, people weren't buying super expensive Cookers for their home. What David Ogilvy was able to do was to go door-to-door and figure out through a process of elimination, which pitches, which advertising pitches, would not get the sale, but would get him through the door. Like that was his first goal. How can I knock on someone's door? First of all, decide are they a good prospect? And then how do I get into their house? And then from there, how do I get into their kitchen? Because once he got into the kitchen, he could get the sale. Now think about that in terms of your funnel. When you run your ad, you're not trying to transact as soon as you present the ad as like, hey, give me some money, I'll give you this product or service. And that's what David understood. And that's what I build into my ads and my funnels, and I'll show you through the BPM method, is it's step by step. First of all, it's not about getting as many people to click as possible. It's not about getting your ad in front of as many people as possible. And again, this quote from David Ogilvy, I think is quite important, is don't count the people that you reach, reach the people that count. And the advertising strategies that I'll share with you build on this. My goal isn't to get your ad in front of millions and millions of people and say, hey, what a great job you've done. You've got low costs in terms of reaching millions of people, but then you don't have any sales. You don't have a return on your ad spend at the end of it. And we can be selective about this regardless of what platform we're on, because we understand human behavior. And if we don't, and if you don't, that's something you will learn through the BPM method. And it's a core part of how I'm able to get deep into the consumer's mindset, is being able to understand some of the core levers of what drives people to take action. And this is a quote from Aristotle, a famous philosopher. All human actions have one or more of these seven causes. Chance, nature, compulsion, habit, reason, passion, and desire. Compulsion is a big one for the BPM method, because if we can create an ad that really appeals to what people want or need or desire, then we can create compulsion. That creates that magnetism where someone sees an ad and immediately they're engaged.

## [06:03 - 09:05]

Then they're interested. Then they have desire and then they take action. That's the aid of funnel. That's something which I'll also explain through the BPM method on how to take that principle from carving out your avatar to getting that into an ad, into a funnel, and a creative message all the way through, so that you can create compulsion. So that when someone sees your ad, when the right person sees your ad, they will be compelled to take action. And a lot of this has been also built from one of the models which I've referenced many, many years called Maslow's hierarchy of needs. And it looks at five parts of what human needs look like. So right at the bottom, we have the basic needs, food, water, and sleep. And think of these as layers. So when you have food, water, sleep, it's easy to think about the next layer, which is safety security resources. Do I have a house? Do I have money, et cetera? Then comes the next layer, which is friendship and love and relationship and things like that. And your network. And then you have another layer when we talk about esteem, so achievement, respect, and confidence. And then once we've got all those layers stacked up, it's easier to think a bit more holistically. How can I help other people? How can I improve the world? How can I leave a legacy? How can I make as much money as I can? And all those kind of things need strong layers. And when we think about advertising, your product or your service will sit at one of those, or more, of those layers. So let's say you're selling bottled water. It's a very basic need. You don't need to explain what water is, but you're gonna have to explain why your water's better than someone else's. And probably there's gonna be a lot more competition there. If you're selling something on, how to leave a legacy in your business, that's kind of near the top of the pyramid. And it's easier to sell to someone who has a strong foundation, but at the same time, it's gonna be harder to explain that than a bottle of water. So this helps us to understand how to pitch our product. If we take an example here, this was an ad that was run by David Ogilvy for the car brand Rolls-Royce, which is at the top of the desire levels when it comes to buying cars. And if you think about this, it's a high-end car. If someone has very basic needs that they're worried about, like food, water, sleep, are they gonna buy this car? No. If they have safety and security and money and resource concerns, are they gonna buy this? No. Then we look at the level of esteem, achievement, respect, confidence. That's the type of person that's gonna buy this. That has a strong foundation of the basics of life, the foundations of money and house and things like that. And then we wanna appeal to something that's gonna be meaningful for them to grab their attention. And that's what this ad does. Now, this ad was, you know, what was it, 1959, it says there. ## [09:05 - 12:05]

And that's a big part of what I'll show you in the BPM method. But there's also a flip side to this, which is, you know, if we look at what people react to, and you'll know this, if you watch the news, if you have your ads appearing in any kind of format, certain things will appeal to your brain more than you realize. And this is a quote from another person I've learned a lot from is Daniel Kahneman. The brains of humans contain a mechanism that is designed to give priority to bad news. This goes way, way back, you know, hundreds, you know, thousands, tens of thousands of years when we were constantly in flight or fight mode. So if you imagine, you know, someone says, there's this animal that's coming to attack, that's gonna get your attention more than, hey, I found a new pot of honey in this orchard. It's not quite memorable. It doesn't have the same impact. And that is something, again, we can use in our ads so that when we are presenting our ads, and you know, we think that there are prospects waiting there to become clients or customers, and we just need to present our ad, and they're gonna throw money at us, realities of our consumers are a bit apprehensive. They want to know why your ad should take their attention, what is the purpose of the click and what they're gonna get from it, and we can appeal to them by being a bit more creative and psychological in the way we present our offers and our messages on our ads. And you know, if you look at the core of the BPM method, it's based on three aspects, brand marketing, performance marketing, and then piling that up into how we build our ads. So brand marketing is understanding the consumer, understanding, you know, the various demographics, their behaviors, thinking about their needs, their desires, their reasons they wouldn't buy, and going deep into what makes up a consumer. So I'm not talking about logos and colors and things like that, I'm talking about the consumer. Performance marketing then looks at the ad platform, and that's the big separation here. So branding thinks about product and the consumer, performance marketing thinks about the ad platform, and in between that, you have your creative messages and your ads, and that's what we're binding together so that when you present the ad, it doesn't matter where the ad is presented, it's done in a way that's native, but that is based on the human interaction. Cause at the end of the day, whether you're advertising hundreds of years ago on, you know, just word of mouth, or maybe a hundred years ago in a magazine, or 50 years ago on TV, or even recently on, you know, on different types of advertising platform, that message and how it's communicated to the end user doesn't really change. The human and on the other side hasn't evolved as much over the last a few hundred years, is maybe we think we have. And that's what we try to bring to teach you through the BPMF that the principles, the strategies and the tactics,

## [12:05 - 14:19]

so that when you are delivering your ad, we are taking apart all the technicals, the ad platform specifics, and binding that with what really matters to get to the heart of why someone would wanna click and why they wanna buy. So, you know, if you look at the three players for any ad platform, there's a user, an advertiser, and the ad platform, and we're using data, you know, on our day to day, you know, what the performance metrics, what should we do, after we've figured out the brand aspect of it. And if I give you an example here, so this is from Facebook or Meta, our goal is to match the right ad to the right person at the right time. This is different than a traditional auction because the winner isn't the ad with the highest monetary bid, but the ad that creates the most value. And this is where many of the ad platforms are going is, they wanna retain their user on the platform, but at the same time, they want your money as an advertiser. And that's what you can play on, and that's what the BPM method plays on, is the ad platform wants you to balance that, give the user a great value, at the same time, they will give you lower cost and higher conversions. And that's really what I built into the BPM method engine. So what you're about to go through is broken up into five core areas. The AC4, Advertising Core 4, which is the success path for ads domination. Once you've gone through this, everything will make sense in terms of how to pitch your products, your audience, your creative offer and your funnel, which is then broken out. And then how to build strong offers. Offers for me are the connection between your audience and your product or your service. That's where your offer sit. Offer could just be an ad with a great message. Offer could be a promotion. It's, you know, that's irrelevant. It's the connecting bridge. And then once you've got that connected, it's to push them through a high converting and highly profitable funnel.
